coredata.dc:title: Portable multispectral fluorometer for determination of formalin in food samples using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Singseeta, Warut
Wongsing, Budsakorn
Naksen, Puttaraksa
Jarujamrus, Purim
Pencharee, Somkid
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a portable multispectral fluorometer for determining formalin (FA). We used nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the fluorescence probe, based on right-angle fluorescence spectrometry with twin excitation high-power light emitting diode sources. The multispectral spectroscopy sensor was used as a detector for fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity values were displayed from 0 to 65535 a.u. The FA determination results show a linear relationship in the FA concentration range of 10-75 mg L−1 with r2 = 0.9908. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.09 mg L−1 (calculated from 3SDblank/slope (n = 3)). In addition, the percentage of relative errors compared with the standard method and standard instrument shows less than 10 percent. The performance of a portable multispectral fluorometer in actual samples exhibited no significant difference compared to the validation instrument results. Therefore, the development of a portable multispectral fluorometer can be used as a fluorometer, and the measurement performance is comparable to a standard fluorescence spectrometer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable multispectral fluorometer
Formalin
High-power-light-emitting diode
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Silver mirror reaction

Validation of the portable multispectral fluorometer measurement
Effect of temperature on fluorescence spectroscopy
Effect of temperature in fluorescence spectroscopy
Performance of the portable multispectral fluorometer
The effects of inter-day analysis and interference
Determination of FA in real food samples and method validation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The composition of the portable multispectral fluorometer (a) the equipment set up, (b) the optical design, and (c) an overview of the link between the portable multispectral fluorometer and laptop.
Thermal effect of the LEDs (a) spectrum of HP-LED (n=3) (b) decreasing of light intensity of the HP-LED (n=3).
(a) The temperature of the 2-mL DI water (n=3) (b) effect of temperature on fluorescence spectroscopy (n=3).

Fluorescence intensity (the excitation wavelength of 365 nm) of (a) DI water, 0.1 M H2S04 without quinine sulfate, and with 0.074 and 0.148 mg L−1 quinine sulfate, respectively (b) DI water, N-CDs, N CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent, and N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent -100 mg L−1 FA, respectively.
Graph showing the relationship between I-I0 and FA concentration (n=3).
Determination of FA in real samples using the developed multispectral fluorometer.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculation of % relative error: % relative error = ([found concentration obtained from the portable multispectral fluorometer - found concentration obtained from Acetylacetone method]/found concentration obtained from Acetylacetone method) × 100.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculation of % relative error: % relative error = ([found concentration obtained from the portable multispectral fluorometer - found concentration obtained from fluorescence spectrophotometer]/found concentration obtained from fluorescence spectrophotometer) × 100.

head.ce:title: Portable multispectral fluorometer for determination of formalin in food samples using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Ubon Ratchathani 34190, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials Science, Sensors & Catalysis for Problem-Based Projects, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Ubon Ratchathani 34190, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cNanomaterials Science, Sensors & Catalysis for Problem-Based Projects, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Ubon Ratchathani 34190, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a portable multispectral fluorometer for determining formalin (FA). We used nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the fluorescence probe, based on right-angle fluorescence spectrometry with twin excitation high-power light emitting diode sources. The multispectral spectroscopy sensor was used as a detector for fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity values were displayed from 0 to 65535 a.u. The FA determination results show a linear relationship in the FA concentration range of 10-75 mg L−1 with r2 = 0.9908. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.09 mg L−1 (calculated from 3SDblank/slope (n = 3)). In addition, the percentage of relative errors compared with the standard method and standard instrument shows less than 10 percent. The performance of a portable multispectral fluorometer in actual samples exhibited no significant difference compared to the validation instrument results. Therefore, the development of a portable multispectral fluorometer can be used as a fluorometer, and the measurement performance is comparable to a standard fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is a suitable analytical technique because of its extreme sensitivity and excellent specificity. It is routinely used for fluorescence detection of changes in conjugated systems, aromatic molecules, and rigidity due to alterations in temperature, pH, ionic strength, solvent, and ligands. Fluorescence spectroscopy lacks interferences, and higher-order data can be obtained from it. In addition, fluorescent compounds are susceptible to their environments at the molecular level. Hence, fluorescence spectroscopy can also provide an accurate tool for monitoring molecular changes while handling, processing, or storing fish and other seafood. However, there is a strong dependence on light scatter, and there are no means for making mathematical corrections because no information on the amount of scatter is contained in the spectrum [1]. Additionally, the fluorescence spectrometer has other limitations; the equipment is expensive and quite bulky. There is also a need for a skilled operator.

Therefore, researchers have been working to develop a low-cost fluorometer that includes two main parts: detection and excitation. Detection commonly uses a photomultiplier tube (PMT) in the highest sensitivity detectors [2,3]. However, this is high-cost, large, and must be operated at high voltage [4]. Therefore, next-generation photodiodes are used as fluorescence detectors in preference to PMTs; they are rugged, low cost, small, and have low power consumption [5,6]. Their limitation is that wavelength selection requires an external optical filter for a wide range of wavelengths. A color sensor based on a photodiode array serves as the fluorescence detector [7,8]; it can detect the received color intensity for red, blue, and green under three integrated optical filters. However, the color sensor does not determine the specific wavelength. Recently, a multispectral spectroscopy sensor (the AS72653), based on a photodiode array detector with an integrated filter, was introduced to detect light intensity at various wavelengths [9]. Moreover, this sensor was developed as a portable spectrometer to observe the weak radiation interaction in mango and apple samples [10,11]. Therefore, we studied this sensor type as a fluorescence-based sensor for formalin (FA) determination.

For excitation, a xenon arc lamp is typically used as a light source in a fluorometer because of its stable intensity from 200 to 800 nm, covering the UV/vis absorption range. However, it is long in size, expensive, and unsuitable for portable fluorometers. Recently, a small light-emitting diode (LED) was applied as an excitation light in a portable fluorometer [2,3,5,7,8]. The small size of the LED, its low power, and low cost made it a good choice. Nevertheless, its low-power usage means that it provides low-light intensity. The intensity of excitation light is one of the factors determining the fluorescence intensity [12]. Therefore, selecting a high-power light-emitting-diode (HP-LED) as the excitation light source [6] could produce sufficient fluorescence intensity. Greater excitation intensities based on HP-LEDs as the excitation light source are expected to generate higher fluorescence intensity levels.

We designed and fabricated a novel multispectral sensing fluorometer based on right-angle fluorescence spectroscopy. Information on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and their synthesis was demonstrated in A.1-2. The excitation wavelength of N-CDs is 310 – 390 nm, so the HP-LEDs at 365 nm were selected as twin excitation light sources. The AS72653 sensor was rationally selected as a detector because it can detect multispectral signals by the corresponding fluorescence wavelength peak of N-CDs at 410 nm. To test this in actual use, the developed system was also applied to determine formalin (FA) using N-CDs as the fluorescence probe in our previous work [13]. For FA determination, the strong blue emission of N-CDs was quenched after adding the silver ion (Ag+) and Tollens’ reagent. This led to the formation of the [Ag(NH3)2]+ complex, incorporated on the surface of the N-CDs. After that, the reduction of Ag+ formed metallic Ag0. This was released from the N-CDs surface in the presence of FA, resulting in increased N-CDs fluorescence with increasing FA concentration. Finally, the multispectral-sensor-based fluorometer using right-angle fluorescence spectroscopy was applied to determine the FA concentration in real food samples. Additionally, a comparison using a fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to verify the constructed portable device's accuracy. Our novel multispectral-sensor-based fluorometer based on right-angle fluorescence spectroscopy, featuring N-CDs as the fluorescence probe, proved to be an effective, accurate, cost-efficient, and reproducible system for quantifying FA in food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Components of the portable multispectral fluorometer
ce:section.ce:para: UV flashlights (SV38, AloneFire, Shenzhen, China), consisting of HP-LED, LED driver, and UV filters, were modified to serve as the excitation light sources. An aluminum heat sink (ILA-HSINK-STAR-50 × 40MM-BLK-K, Intelligent LED Solutions, Thatcham, UK) was used for the thermal management of the LEDs. A multispectral spectroscopy sensor (AS72563, SparkFun Electronics, Colorado, USA) was chosen to develop a fluorescence detector for the portable multispectral fluorometer. An Arduino microcontroller (Uno, Arduino, Torino, Italy) was used as a processor. A plano-convex glass lens (F50, Gammaco, Nonthaburi, Thailand) served as an excitation lens, and a convex glass lens (F25, Gammaco, Nonthaburi, Thailand) was the emission lens. A generic power supply adapter (12V, 5A, 60W) transformer for the LED strip was used to power the portable multispectral fluorometer.

A 3D printer (model i3 Mega S, Anycubic, Shenzhen, China) was used to create the sample holder, excitation, and detection parts using a black filament (PLA+). A miniature spectrometer (model OCEANFX, Ocean Insight, Florida, USA) was used to measure the validation device for the HP-LED. The fluorescence spectrometer (model LS55; PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA) was used to measure the fluorescence emission of the samples for method validation. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) operating within a spectral range of 200-800 nm was used to measure the absorbance for validation by the acetylacetone method.

The portable multispectral fluorometer was created based on right-angle fluorescence spectroscopy. All parts were packed in a black box (200 × 120 × 75 mm3) (Fig. 1 a). The potable multispectral fluorometer could be used according to the steps in Fig. A1-A3 (Supplementary material), and the operational flowchart is described in Fig. A4 (Supplementary material).

For designing the excitation system, we used twin light sources to increase the intensity of the light beam. The excitation lights were modified from two UV flashlights, including HP-LEDs, UV filters, and drivers. Two similar sets of HP-LEDs with heatsinks (a diameter of 50 mm and a length of 40 mm), plano-convex lenses (6 mm thickness, 25 mm diameter, and 50 mm focal length), and UV filters (300 - 400 nm bandpass, 20 mm diameter, and 2 mm thickness) were packed in a created excitation-3D socket. Then, the plano-convex lens was placed in front of the HP-LED at 25 mm to change the beam from diffused to parallel. A 10 mm UV filter was seated next to the lens to block light noise (Fig. 1b). The sample holder was designed and created with 3 × 1 cm2 channels for the two excitation-light beams and the fluorescence beam.

The detection component was created based on an AS72563 sensor, used to detect fluorescence intensity in the visible wavelengths at 410, 435, 460, 485, 510, and 535 nm, each with 20 nm of full-width at half-max detection. A detection-3D socket was designed to use the AS72563 sensor with a convex lens. The convex lens was placed in front of the AS72653 sensor at a 20-mm distance to focus the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1b). The sensor was interfaced with the Arduino microcontroller via synchronous serial communication with an Inter IC Communication (I2C) for data transmission.

For the display, the screen monitor on the Nextion-LCD module was created to display the real-time digital fluorescence intensity data every 10 seconds, transmitted from the Arduino microcontroller via a universal asynchronous receiver transmitter (UART). The serial monitor program on the laptop was used to record this data from the portable multispectral fluorometer via Universal Serial Bus communication (USB) by a transfer rate (baud rate) of 9600 bps (Fig. 1c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the portable multispectral fluorometer measurement

ce:section.ce:para: The HP-LED was investigated with a heat sink to determine the suitable duration of activation. The spectrum and light intensity of HP-LEDs were measured at room temperature (25°C) every 5 minutes for 60 minutes by an OCEANFX Miniature spectrometer. The light intensity was plotted on the y-axis against time on the x-axis. This approach allowed us to study the relationship between the light intensity and the time the HP-LEDs were switched on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: HP-LEDs continuously irradiated the 2 mL deionized (DI) water to investigate the rate of the temperature increase of the solution. The temperature was recorded every 10 seconds for 50 minutes and then plotted on the y-axis against the duration on the x-axis.

The following approach was used to investigate the effect of temperature on fluorescence spectroscopy using N-CDs, based on the silver mirror reaction [13]: N-CDs (250 mg L−1), Ag+ (0.0625 M), and Tollens’ reagent (0.40 mM) were combined in a 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tube. The standard formalin solution in concentrations of 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg L−1 was then added to the mixed solution and incubated for 3 minutes at room temperature. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 minutes to eliminate the Ag0 precipitate and preserve the clear supernatant solution; the mixture was again centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 minutes. After that, a 2-mL clear solution was obtained, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at 410 nm every 10 seconds for 3 minutes. The fluorescence intensity was plotted on the y-axis against duration on the x-axis.

Actual food samples, including shrimp, crispy squid, and bean sprouts, were cut into small pieces to determine FA. First, a 5.0 g aliquot of each sample was soaked with 50 mL of DI water, followed by incubation for 30 min. Next, the mixture was filtrated twice through a Whatman No.1 filter paper. After that, each solution of the as-prepared food samples was diluted 100-fold and spiked with concentrations of standard formaldehyde solution at 0, 25, and 50 mg L−1. Then, 750 µL of the obtained liquid sample was analyzed using the same procedure reported in Section 2.4.2.

The thermal effect was studied to determine a suitable warm-up time. First, the light intensity of the HP-LED was measured while turning it on (Fig. 2 a). After running for 60 minutes, the light intensity dropped by around 5 percent (Fig. 2b). The increase in temperature of the HP-LED was responsible for this loss of intensity. In addition, after turning the lights on and running for 50 minutes, the decrease in the light intensity was very slight. Therefore, this time allowed the lights to warm up before use (Fig. A1-A3, Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature in fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on fluorescence spectroscopy was studied to select the optimum measurement time. The water temperature increased from 25.5 to 39.3°C in 50 minutes because of the high-temperature light beam (Fig. 3 a). According to the literature, high temperatures can affect energy loss by non-radiation. Thus, the temperature rose rapidly from 25.5°C to about 30°C, which is suitable for solution-based fluorescence spectroscopy [14–18]. At 0-180 s, the temperature is less than 30°C. Therefore, these conditions are used for investigating the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 410 nm with formalin-added concentrations of 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg L−1, as shown in Fig. 3b. As seen in the figure, the fluorescence intensity rate at each concentration tended to fall significantly over time. The raised temperature caused the fluorescence spectrum to fall in intensity. Hence, our portable multispectral fluorometer's optimum measurement time is 0-30 s for average fluorescence intensity for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the portable multispectral fluorometer
ce:section.ce:para: The portable multispectral fluorometer performance was used to detect a specific group's fluorescence intensity. The quinine sulfate dihydrate solution and the proposed FA determination using N-CDs as the fluorescence probe were selected as the model molecule in this study.

The excitation and emission wavelength peaks of quinine sulfate dihydrate solution are 320 to 380 and 450 nm [19], respectively (Fig. 4 a). The DI water, 0.1 M H2SO4, 0.074 and 0.148 mg L−1 of quinine sulfate were measured for fluorescence intensity using the portable multispectral fluorometer. The result shows how fluorescence intensity increased when increasing the quinine sulfate concentration. This confirms that the portable multispectral fluorometer can determine the fluorescence of the model compound.

Moreover, we selected the FA determination based on the traditional silver mirror reaction, using N-CDs as a fluorescence probe. First, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs showed a solid blue emission at wavelength 410 nm when excited at 330 nm (turn on). Then, with the cover of Ag+ and Tollens’ reagent on the surface of N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased (turn off). The fluorescence switch-back after FA addition (turn on) is shown in Fig. 4b. This result confirms that the proposed fluorescence spectrometer can measure the model reaction according to our previous report [13].

The study of the linearity range for FA determination was performed using the optimum conditions (Fig. A5, Supplementary material) (250 mg L−1 N-CDs, 0.0625 M Ag+, and 0.40 mM Tollens’ reagent). The plot of FA concentration against the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm is shown in Fig. 5 (I-I0; I and I0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of FA, respectively). The results show that the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm increases with FA concentration. A linear relationship for FA determination using the portable multispectral fluorometer was obtained in the concentration range of 10-75 mg L−1, with r2 = 0.9908. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.09 mg L−1, calculated from 3SDblank/slope (n=3). Our device shows comparable LOD for FA determination and a relatively short response time, as shown in Table A3 in the Supplementary material. Therefore, our fluorometer can determine another target molecule with an excitation wavelength in the same range as our light source. Moreover, the LOD found in this work is comparable to that in a previous report [13]. These results indicate that the portable multispectral fluorometer is applicable for quantitatively determining FA with high sensitivity and portability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of inter-day analysis and interference
ce:section.ce:para: N-CD stability for determining FA concentration was also investigated by performing inter-day experiments (Table A1, Supplementary material). The result shows that the %RSD of inter-day was 0.18. This result suggests that the reproducibility of the proposed portable multispectral fluorometer for determining FA was acceptable. A study of the interference effects of acetaldehyde, benzaldehyde, and acetylacetone disturbances on formaldehyde determinations [20]. We found that glucose, formic acid, and acetone would be contaminated. Our method could withstand interference with different concentration ratios (Table A2, Supplementary material) compared to interference-free FA. Our approach is resistant to interference when the interfering concentration is within a range observed in the real sample and the signal difference is within an acceptable range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FA in real food samples and method validation
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the silver mirror reaction, the portable multispectral fluorometer was further used to investigate FA contamination in food samples. The food samples included shrimp, crispy squid, and green sprouts. To evaluate the accuracy of the portable multispectral fluorometer, each piece was spiked with a standard FA solution. The portable multispectral fluorometer determined the FA concentration, and we compared this with the un-spiked samples. In addition, the results obtained from the fluorometer were also compared to those measured by the classical acetylacetone method [21] and on the fluorescence spectrophotometer based on the silver mirror reaction. The results showed that the recovery (% recovery) was in the range of 95.02-110.07%, and the relative error (% relative error) was less than 5% across all samples (Table 1 ), suggesting acceptable accuracy by the portable multispectral fluorometer

We successfully demonstrated the novel portable multispectral fluorometer for indirectly reporting the concentration of FA on a solution system as preliminary data before analysis using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The proposed portable multispectral fluorometer consisted of newly introduced twin excitation components and a detection component that can detect six wavelengths of fluorescence intensity. The proposed system shows that the performance is similar to a traditional fluorometer. Moreover, the novel system showed the possibility of fluorescence detection of the model fluorescence compound and FA determination using N-CDs as a fluorescence probe. The advantages of the developed system are its portability, multispectral operation, and that it is inexpensive. Future developments could improve its performance for other targets and techniques by changing the excitation light source and detector.

Warut Singseeta: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Budsakorn Wongsing: Resources, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Puttaraksa Naksen: Resources, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Purim Jarujamrus: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Somkid Pencharee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Department of Physics and Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, is recognized for its instrumental facilities. We are also grateful for the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) awarded to Warut Singseeta. We would also like to acknowledge The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC) and the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research, and Innovation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2023.100199.

J.R. Lakowicz. Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy (3rd Ed.). (2006).

Actuators B 182 (2013) 416-423. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2013.03.052

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hand-held electronic tongue based on fluorometry for taste assessment of tea

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic color filters integrated on a photodiode array

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A portable spectrometric system for quantitatively p the soluble solids content of apples with a pre-calibrated multispectral sensor chipset

IEEE Access. 8 (2020) 212371-212382. https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2020.3040062

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiphoton excitation of fluorescent DNA base analogs

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Old silver mirror in qualitative analysis with new shoots in quantification: nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as fluorescent probes for “off-on” sensing of formalin in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Naksen, P. Jarujamrus, W. Anutrasakda, V. Promarak, L. Zhang, W. Shen, Old silver mirror in qualitative analysis with new shoots in quantification: nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as fluorescent probes for “off-on” sensing of formalin in food samples. Talanta. 236 (2021) 122862. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122862

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Warut Singseeta is currently a Ph.D. candidate at the Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand. He received his master's degree (2017) in Physics from Ubon Ratchathani University. His research interest mainly focused on the development of portable fluorometers.

Budsakorn Wongsing obtained a Master's degree in Chemistry from, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand. Her research interest mainly focused on the development of nanomaterials for microfluidic paper-based analytical devices and their applications.

Puttaraksa Naksen is currently a Ph.D. student at the Department of chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand. She received her bachelor's degree (2017) in Chemistry from Ubon Ratchathani University. Her research interest mainly focused on the development of nanomaterials-based methodologies and their applications.

Purim Jarujamrus is an Associate Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University. His research is focused on the development of functional nanocomposites on fiber-based microfluidics analytical devices for novel chemical and bio-sensing and spectroanalytical chemistry. To date, he has published ∼72 articles in peer-reviewed journals.

Somkid Pencharee is an Assistant Professor at the Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University. His research is focused on Thermoelectricity Single, Crystal Spectroscopy, and Computer Interfacing. To date, he has published ∼24 articles in peer-reviewed journals.
